title: All-frames subframes can take over tab-level desktop media state and control routing
source: attacker-controlled same-tab subframe DOM/video metadata and media events
broken_control: media state and desktop control routing are tab-scoped while content scripts run in all frames
sink: desktop subtitle fetch via yt-dlp and extension control routing to preferred frame
impact: subframe can replace active tab media, trigger desktop network fetches for attacker URLs, and receive user playback controls
why_plausible: manifest all_frames injects content script into subframes; background stores media by tabId and preferred frame; desktop contract lacks frameId/origin/visibility binding
closest_control: real media checks and HTTP(S)-only URL filtering do not bind the selected media source to top frame, visible frame, frame origin, or a frame-aware desktop contract

title: Blacklist URL changes are broadcast before blacklist re-evaluation
sink: extension background broadcasts URL/title to desktop WebSocket and active desktop state
impact: one blacklisted same-origin/path/hash URL and title can leak after navigation
why_plausible: static trace confirms initial blacklisted loads are suppressed but active URLWatcher events send first
closest_control: evaluateCurrentUrl stops monitoring after the send; it is ordered too late for the triggering navigation event

title: Plugin network allowlist can be bypassed by redirects to unallowed hosts
source: plugin-controlled usp.fetch URL to a manifest/config-allowed host that returns an HTTP redirect
broken_control: the sandbox validates only the original URL host before calling default redirect-following fetch
sink: host fetch follows redirect and returns final response body/URL/headers to the plugin
impact: a plugin with network permission for one allowed host can reach arbitrary final destinations, including local/LAN/cloud-metadata-style hosts if an allowed host redirects there
why_plausible: Node fetch follows redirects by default; a local probe showed default fetch returned a 200 response from the redirected final URL; no final response URL host check exists
closest_control: allowedNetworkHosts contains manifest and config-derived hosts, but only getFetchHost(input) is checked before the fetch call

title: Any local browser extension origin can connect to the loopback desktop control WebSocket without the token
source: any installed local extension able to open ws://127.0.0.1:44501 with an Origin matching chrome-extension:// or moz-extension://
broken_control: loopback path treats all extension origins as trusted and skips the per-install authToken entirely
sink: desktop accepts usp-extension messages, updates active media state, downloads subtitles for supplied HTTP(S) URLs, and later sends control commands to the claimed tab socket
impact: a malicious or compromised local extension can impersonate the real extension to drive the desktop app and coerce local network/subtitle fetch behavior without knowing the configured token
why_plausible: connectionAuth.ts returns true for loopback after broad extension-origin regex; tests assert this tokenless loopback behavior; default endpoint builder omits tokens for loopback
closest_control: ordinary web origins are rejected and non-loopback requires token, but there is no extension-id allowlist or loopback token requirement

title: yt-dlp updater downloads and executes release assets without checksum or signature verification
source: GitHub releases API response and selected asset browser_download_url
broken_control: release asset URL and bytes are trusted after HTTPS fetch, without comparing a pinned checksum/signature/attestation from a separate trusted source
sink: downloaded bytes are written to userData, chmodded executable, and later spawned for subtitle and transcription jobs
impact: a compromised release API response, release asset, CDN/TLS trust break, or malicious upstream release can persist arbitrary executable code under the app's userData and run with user privileges
why_plausible: code selects asset URL from API JSON, downloads bytes, writes/renames targetPath, chmods 0755, and spawn() later executes that path; no digest/signature verification is present
closest_control: HTTPS and GitHub API asset name selection reduce accidental wrong downloads but do not provide content integrity for executable update assets

title: Manifest-controlled file setting defaults can self-grant readSelectedFile access

affected_locations.label: source
affected_locations.evidence: file setting defaultValue accepts any string

affected_locations.label: source
affected_locations.evidence: install materializes manifest setting defaults into plugin config

affected_locations.label: root_control
affected_locations.evidence: readableFiles are derived from config values for manifest file fields

affected_locations.label: sink
affected_locations.evidence: usp.readFile gates readSelectedFile then reads exact allowed targetPath

attacker_controlled_source: A malicious plugin manifest/package can declare settingsSchema with a file field defaultValue such as an absolute local path; renderer/settings updates can also set the schema field.
broken_control: The selected-file allowlist treats manifest-provided/default config as a selected file without evidence of a user file-picker selection.
sink: fs.readFile(targetPath, "utf-8") in pluginSandbox.ts:464 after exact readableFiles membership.
impact: A plugin with readSelectedFile can read known local files without the user selecting them; with a network path or provider output it can disclose the contents across the plugin boundary.
closest_control: PluginPermissionGate.require("readSelectedFile") and exact normalized path membership in pluginSandbox.ts:459-464; incomplete because PluginManager seeds readableFiles from config/defaults at pluginManager.ts:581-590.
validation_evidence: Source trace shows validateSettingsDefaultValue accepts string defaults for file fields at pluginManifest.ts:257-261, ensurePluginConfig writes defaults at pluginManager.ts:477-489 and defaultConfigFromSettings copies defaultValue at pluginManager.ts:508-520, getReadableFiles grants config file values at pluginManager.ts:581-590, and sandbox readFile consumes that grant at pluginSandbox.ts:457-465. Focused pluginManager derived-grants test passed and existing sandbox tests prove the sink reads allowed readableFiles and denies only when the set is empty.
attack_path_facts: Attacker controls plugin manifest during plugin install/update source review.
Plugin needs readSelectedFile permission but does not need the user to select the specific default path.
The target path must be predictable or supplied through renderer/settings config.
Reading occurs with the desktop app user filesystem privileges.

title: Plugin config URL strings can silently expand the network host allowlist

affected_locations.label: source
affected_locations.evidence: string/select/textarea/file defaultValue accepts URL-shaped strings and serverList defaults accept serverUrl strings at lines 235-249

affected_locations.label: source
affected_locations.evidence: plugin config updates are accepted as arbitrary objects without schema/key validation

affected_locations.label: root_control
affected_locations.evidence: allowedNetworkHosts are manifest hosts plus every URL-shaped string found recursively in config

affected_locations.label: sink
affected_locations.evidence: usp.fetch permits any request whose initial host is in allowedNetworkHosts

attacker_controlled_source: A malicious plugin manifest can place URL-shaped strings in default config values, including serverList default serverUrl entries, and a renderer/settings update can place URL-shaped strings in plugin config.
broken_control: Network host grants are inferred from all config strings, not only explicit manifest.network.allowedHosts or user-selected server URL fields.
sink: fetch(input, parseFetchInit(...)) in pluginSandbox.ts:474 when the derived host is present in allowedNetworkHosts.
impact: A plugin with network permission can fetch undeclared localhost, LAN, or other sensitive hosts by seeding or obtaining URL-shaped config values that were not declared in manifest.network.allowedHosts.
closest_control: PluginPermissionGate.require("network") at pluginSandbox.ts:468 and host set membership at pluginSandbox.ts:470-473; incomplete because pluginManager.ts:541-579 expands the host set from arbitrary config values before the sandbox sees it.
validation_evidence: Source trace shows getAllowedNetworkHosts adds manifest hosts then calls collectNetworkHosts(config) at pluginManager.ts:541-550; collectNetworkHostFromString adds any URL.canParse host at pluginManager.ts:571-579; runtime start and refresh pass these grants at pluginManager.ts:334-340 and 280-284; sandbox fetch checks only that derived set at pluginSandbox.ts:466-475. Focused pluginManager derived-grants test passed, confirming config-derived grants are active behavior.
attack_path_facts: Attacker needs a plugin with network permission.
The host can be supplied by manifest default settings during install or by renderer/settings config later.
Official serverList behavior shows dynamic user-entered hosts are intended, but the current collector is field-agnostic and default-aware.
The fetch runs in the utility process with desktop network reachability.
